blocks.content: Eight years ago. A bag on a kitchen counter, zipper half-open. Someone asked for the charger — said it was in the front pocket.
My own handwriting. Front pocket, folded in quarters, gone soft at the folds.
I had written that letter slowly. I chose every word. I gave it to the one person I trusted to make sure it arrived.
I had been waiting for an answer for weeks. Then months. I told myself the silence was the answer. What else was it going to be.
The charger was there too. Right underneath.
I put the letter back. I handed the charger over. I smiled. No one noticed anything.
Eight years. I sat across tables. I flew to things I had no reason to fly to. I watched the life I asked for in that letter slowly become someone else's, piece by piece, quietly.
The whole time I thought I understood why. I thought he'd made his choice. It was in the pocket the whole time.
What was I supposed to say? There were people in the next room. It had been eight years. What sentence starts that conversation?
I drove home that night and sat in my car for a long time. I went back through every dinner, every flight, every time I convinced myself it didn't matter. All of it.

blocks.prompt: A pocket. Eight years. What stays with you?

options.label: The letter was in there the whole time.
Everything built on top of a silence that was never an answer.
Put it back. Hand over the charger. Smile.
